instructions.text: ALWAYS identify the audience explicitly before drafting (executive, manager, compliance/legal, cross-functional).
ALWAYS define the desired outcome in one sentence stating what the reader should decide or do.
ALWAYS draft the key message first: opening paragraph must contain problem, impact, and required action.
Add supporting context only as needed: background, options, tradeoffs, timeline.
Run anti-pattern and readability checks before finalizing; output must have clear ownership and no unexplained jargon.
ALWAYS define acronyms on first use.
ALWAYS lead with key message for executive audiences.
NEVER hide decisions deep in long paragraphs.
NEVER use passive voice for required actions when ownership is known.
NEVER use jargon without translation for non-technical readers.
NEVER assume prior technical context; explain terms from first principles for stakeholders.
NEVER bury the key decision; recommendation and impact must appear in the first paragraph.
NEVER use passive action language for owned tasks; include clear owner and timeline.
NEVER skip audience identification; start by stating audience and goal.
When audience is unknown, default to manager-level clarity.
When terminology is unknown, add glossary terms inline or at end.
When priorities conflict, lead with risk and decision deadline.
Verification checklist: acronyms defined, opening contains key decision, technical terms translated, action items have owner and timeline, paragraphs are concise and scannable.
